topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent hum of the library felt like a physical assault. I was hunched over my laptop, the screen illuminating the familiar, sickeningly perfect prose of an essay I’d painstakingly crafted for my application. Then I saw it. Tucked between two unrelated submissions, a file labeled simply, "Sample: Impact Essay." My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that tasted like dust. It was my essay. Every carefully chosen metaphor, every vulnerable admission, laid bare for anyone to dissect. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outwards until my hands began to tremble uncontrollably. I slowly closed the file, the click of the lid echoing too loudly in the silence. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding like a declaration of defeat. I needed air, badly.

[story 2]

It was the sheer audacity of it that made my jaw clench. I found the essay buried deep within an archived folder—my own words, polished and presented as some kind of universal blueprint. Reading it felt like watching someone else wear my skin, speaking my intentions with an effortless grace I hadn't managed to capture myself. My shoulders slumped, the tension in my neck becoming unbearable. I reread the opening paragraph, the words mocking me with their clarity. A slow, burning sensation rose in my chest, hot and suffocating. I didn't move for a long time, just staring at the screen, my gaze tracing the lines of the expertly constructed argument, feeling utterly hollowed out. When I finally stood up, my movements were stiff, deliberate, as if every muscle was fighting an invisible weight.

[story 3]

The discovery hit me with a dull thud, like a dropped stone in deep water. I stared at the screen, my vision blurring slightly. It was mine. The narrative arc, the insightful reflection—it was all there, cataloged and approved, waiting for some future applicant to borrow and repurpose. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I felt a sudden, intense need to crumple the paper, to destroy the evidence of my effort, but my fingers remained stubbornly still above the keyboard. Instead, I let out a short, humorless laugh that caught in my throat. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, the posture radiating a distinct sense of being profoundly wronged. I just wanted it gone.
